---
title: "FAQ"
pagetitle: "FAQ — EM 619 MLPA"
---

## Course Logistics

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## How are the sessions conducted?
Sessions are **online over Zoom** and recorded. Live timings: **Wednesday 7:45–9:15 PM** and **Saturday 11:00 AM–12:30 PM**. Recordings are posted on the course channel.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Who teaches the course?
The course is taught by **Prof. Nipun Batra**, supported by TAs **Nayan Solanki** and **Balbir Prasad**. See the [Home page](index.qmd#course-information) for contact details.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## What programming language and tools do we use?
**Python**, with libraries such as **NumPy, pandas, scikit-learn**, and **Keras 3.0** for neural networks. Most assignments require programming.
:::

---

## Prerequisites

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## What background do I need?
There are no hard prerequisites, but comfort with **Python**, **linear algebra**, **calculus**, and **basic probability & statistics** will help. See the [preparation materials](index.qmd#preparation-materials) on the Home page to brush up.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## I haven't taken a formal ML course. Can I still take this?
Yes. The course reviews the necessary foundations before moving to advanced topics — but expect to put in extra effort in the first few weeks.
:::

---

## Assignments

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Are assignments individual or group work?
**Groups of 2.** Each assignment is followed by a **viva voce** where both members should be able to explain the submitted work.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Can I discuss assignments with others?
Yes — you are **encouraged** to discuss problems with peers, TAs, and instructors **before** submission. The final submission must be your group's own work.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## How many assignments are there?
**3 assignments**, each worth **15%** (45% total), done in **groups of 2** and followed by a viva. See [Evaluation](grading.qmd) for the full breakdown.
:::

---

## Quizzes & Exams

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## How many quizzes are there, and what's the format?
**3 quizzes**, each worth **15%** (45% total), with short conceptual questions (one-line, MCQ, or fill-in-the-blank). Quizzes are held during the term and in the **Examination-1** and **Examination-2** windows.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## When are the exam windows?
The academic calendar lists **Examination-1** (10–16 Aug 2026) and **Examination-2** (5–11 Oct 2026); two of the quizzes are held in these windows. See the [key dates](grading.qmd#key-dates-module-3-2026) for the full calendar.
:::

---

## Materials

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Where do I find slides and notebooks?
The [Schedule](schedule.qmd) page links slides and notebooks for each lecture, drawn from the shared [`ml-teaching`](https://github.com/nipunbatra/ml-teaching) repository. Session recordings are posted on the course channel.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Which textbooks should I follow?
The course draws on several references — most prominently *An Introduction to Statistical Learning*, *Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning*, and *Mathematics for Machine Learning*. The full annotated list is on the [Home page](index.qmd#reference-textbooks).
:::
